def: The cash a business keeps tied up in running itself — receivables and inventory, less the payables it hasn't yet paid. Deal practice strips cash and funded debt, so net working capital is current assets less current liabilities excluding both. Growth quietly consumes it, which is why fast-growing companies can be profitable and cash-poor at once.
usage: The purchase agreement pegs a working capital target, and the desk expects a fight over the true-up.

def: A valuation built from first principles: project the cash a business will throw off, discount each year back at a rate reflecting its risk, and add a terminal value for everything beyond the forecast. Intrinsic rather than relative — which makes it only as honest as its assumptions.
usage: The desk ran a DCF alongside the comps, and as usual it said whatever the terminal assumptions told it to say.

def: The arithmetic of whether a transaction raises or lowers the acquirer's pro forma earnings per share. All-stock deals accrete when the buyer's P/E exceeds the price paid for the target's earnings; cash deals when the target's earnings yield beats the after-tax cost of funds. Boards fixate on it, sometimes at the expense of actual value.
usage: The all-stock structure screens dilutive in year one, so the deck leans on synergy-adjusted accretion by year two.

def: The polite term for a private equity firm in its buying role: an investor that acquires companies with pooled fund capital and leverage rather than operating them from a strategic base. What separates the sponsor from a strategic acquirer is the clock — it buys to sell, typically within three to seven years, and underwrites to the exit from day one.
usage: The auction drew four strategics and two sponsors, and the sponsors dropped out the week leverage markets softened.

def: A committed line the borrower can draw, repay, and redraw at will — corporate finance's credit card, priced off SOFR with a fee on the undrawn portion. It sits at the top of the capital structure and exists mostly for working-capital swings; a fully drawn revolver is a classic distress signal.
usage: The company drew $40 million on the revolver to bridge the seasonal inventory build.

def: The single discount rate that equates a bond's price to the present value of every remaining coupon plus principal at redemption. It assumes each coupon is reinvested at that same rate and the bond is held to the end — two assumptions markets rarely honor, which is why realized returns drift from quoted yields.
usage: She quoted the block at a 4.62% yield to maturity and let the dollar price fall out of the calculator.

def: The shares actually available to trade: total shares outstanding minus insider stakes, restricted stock, and strategic holdings that never hit the tape. Thin floats amplify everything — a modest order can move the price disproportionately, and index providers weight constituents by float for precisely this reason.
usage: With barely 15% of the shares in public float, the desk warned the block would take a week to work.

def: The price at which the option's holder may buy (call) or sell (put) the underlying, fixed at trade date and indifferent to where the stock wanders afterward. Everything else about an option — premium, moneyness, the Greeks — is measured against this one contractual number.
usage: The desk rolled the hedge up to a higher strike after the rally left the old one deep in the money.

def: Two rival inflation gauges that rarely agree. CPI, from the BLS, tracks a fixed basket of urban households' out-of-pocket spending; PCE, from the BEA, covers all consumption — including what employers and Medicare pay — with weights that update as buying shifts. The Fed targets 2% on PCE, which typically prints a few tenths cooler thanks to substitution and a smaller shelter weight.
usage: CPI ran hot Tuesday, but the desk expects the PCE translation to land closer to target.

def: The split of a portfolio across stocks, bonds, cash, and alternatives — the decision that drives most of the variance in returns, dwarfing security selection. Get the mix right for the investor's horizon and stomach, and the individual picks matter far less than the brochures suggest.
usage: The adviser spent the whole meeting on allocation and thirty seconds on fund selection, which is roughly the right ratio.

def: The running stream of executed trades — price, size, time — descended from the ticker machines that once printed it. Reading it well means inferring who is buying, who is selling, and with what urgency from the prints alone. When someone says the tape looks heavy, they mean sellers keep showing up regardless of the news.
usage: Don't fight the tape — it's been offered all morning and every bounce keeps getting sold.

def: Cash collected before the product ships or the service runs — a liability, since the company still owes performance, converting to revenue as delivery occurs. Its growth is a leading indicator for subscription businesses, arriving ahead of the income statement. Purchase accounting used to haircut it to fair value, vaporizing acquired revenue, until standard-setters patched the rule in 2021.
usage: Deferred revenue grew 30% against 18% reported revenue, which is the slide the bulls lead with.

def: The blended price of a company's capital: cost of equity and after-tax cost of debt, each weighted by its share of the capital structure at market values. It is the discount rate for unlevered cash flows in a DCF, and small changes in it move the valuation more than most modeling arguments do.
usage: A 50-basis-point cut to WACC did more for the valuation than three days of arguing over revenue growth.

def: The value a combination is supposed to create beyond the sum of its parts — cost savings from consolidated overhead and procurement, or revenue gains from cross-selling. Cost synergies are bankable; revenue synergies are aspirational, and the market discounts them accordingly. Whoever pays a premium for control is effectively prepaying for them.
usage: The buyer's premium only pencils if you credit the full run-rate synergies by year three.

def: The slice of an LBO's purchase price the sponsor covers with fund capital rather than debt — the residual once lenders have set how much leverage they'll tolerate. Somewhere between a third and half of the capitalization depending on the credit cycle, it sits at the bottom of the structure and absorbs the first dollar of loss.
usage: With financing markets shut, the sponsor stretched the equity check to nearly half the capitalization just to get the deal signed.

def: The institutional workhorse of leveraged lending: a floating-rate loan syndicated to CLOs and credit funds rather than held by banks. Amortization is token — typically 1% a year with a bullet at maturity — and covenants are usually light, since the buyers want spread at scale, not quarterly leash-tugging.
usage: The sponsor upsized the Term Loan B by $200 million to fund the dividend.

def: A bond's price sensitivity to yields, expressed in years: modified duration of 7 means roughly a 7% price drop for a 100-basis-point rise in rates. Macaulay duration — the cash-flow-weighted average time to repayment — is the older cousin; low coupons and long maturities stretch both.
usage: With the portfolio running a duration of six, the desk shed the long end before the FOMC.

def: The running total of shares sold short and not yet bought back, usually quoted as a percentage of float or as days-to-cover — short interest divided by average daily volume. High readings signal skepticism, but they also measure the fuel available for a squeeze if the skeptics are wrong.
usage: Days-to-cover crept past eight, and the desk started treating every uptick as a potential stampede.

def: Where the underlying sits relative to the strike: in the money if exercise would pay today, at the money if they roughly coincide, out of the money otherwise. It governs how much of the premium is real value versus hope, and it drives delta, gamma, and the shape of the vol surface.
usage: With the stock pinned near the strike, moneyness flipped hourly and the market-makers earned their keep.

def: Headline inflation with food and energy stripped out — not because groceries don't matter, but because their swings say more about weather and OPEC than about underlying price pressure. Central bankers watch core, and increasingly "supercore" (services excluding housing), as the cleaner read on where inflation settles once the noise washes through.
usage: Headline cooled on gasoline, but core printed 0.3% for a third straight month, so the doves stayed quiet.

def: Selling what has run and buying what has lagged to restore the portfolio's target weights. Mechanically it forces the discipline most investors lack — trimming winners into strength — and without it a 60/40 quietly drifts into 75/25 after a long bull market, carrying more risk than anyone signed up for.
usage: The desk rebalances quarterly and on any five-point drift, whichever comes first.

def: A trade that has actually executed and hit the tape — the only price that counts, as opposed to quotes, which are merely intentions. By extension, any officially released figure: an inflation print, a GDP print. A big block print away from the market often says more than an hour of quoted spreads.
usage: There's a two million share print at the low — someone finally got their exit done.

def: The accounting confession that an acquisition wasn't worth what was paid. When a reporting unit's fair value falls below its carrying amount, goodwill is written down through the income statement — a non-cash charge, tested at least annually, and never reversed under U.S. GAAP. Analysts strip it from earnings; boards still hear about it.
usage: The desk had modeled the write-down two quarters before the auditors made it official.

def: The return shareholders demand for holding a company's stock instead of something safer — unobservable, so it is estimated, usually via CAPM: the risk-free rate plus beta times the equity risk premium. Because equity holders stand last in line, it always exceeds the cost of debt.
usage: With the 10-year backing up, the cost of equity followed, and every DCF on the desk quietly lost value.

def: The post-closing accounting exercise that assigns the purchase price to the target's identifiable assets and liabilities at fair value, with the unexplained remainder booked as goodwill. Write-ups to fixed assets and intangibles create incremental depreciation and amortization, which quietly drags on pro forma earnings for years.
usage: Ask the accountants how much of the step-up lands in amortizable intangibles versus goodwill.

def: Ownership a selling shareholder — usually management — reinvests into the buyout vehicle instead of cashing out at close. Sponsors like it because it keeps operators financially committed and shrinks the check; sellers accept it for a second bite of the apple at the next exit. Typically 10–30% of management's proceeds, tax-deferred if structured properly.
usage: Management rolled forty percent of their proceeds, which told the lenders everything they needed to know about the forecast.

def: Senior and junior debt fused into a single facility with one blended rate and one lender group — the signature product of private credit. Speed and certainty are the sale: one negotiation, no syndication risk. Behind the curtain, an agreement among lenders often splits the tranche into first-out and last-out pieces anyway.
usage: The direct lender committed the whole unitranche in three weeks, which is why the sponsor never called the banks.

def: The curvature duration ignores: as yields fall, prices rise faster than duration predicts, and as yields rise, they fall slower. Positive convexity is a free lunch bondholders pay for in yield; callable and mortgage bonds carry negative convexity, which punishes holders whichever way rates move sharply.
usage: The mortgage book's negative convexity forced the desk to chase duration into the rally.

def: What happens when a heavily shorted stock rallies and the shorts' losses force them to buy back, pushing the price further into the faces of those still short. The mechanics are self-reinforcing: covering demand begets higher prices begets margin calls begets more covering. Fundamentals take a back seat until the crowded side clears.
usage: The move had nothing to do with earnings — it was a squeeze, and the tape said so by mid-morning.

def: An option's premium splits in two: intrinsic value, what exercising would pay right now, and time value, the extra the market charges for what might happen before expiry. A deep in-the-money call is mostly intrinsic; an at-the-money one is nearly all time value, which theta grinds away daily.
usage: The trainee paid up for a week of pure time value and watched it melt over a quiet expiry.

def: The BLS's monthly count of net jobs added outside farming, released the first Friday of most months and capable of repricing the entire rates curve in a second. Markets parse the trinity — payrolls, the unemployment rate, average hourly earnings — and revisions to prior months can matter as much as the print itself.
usage: The desk went home flat Thursday; nobody wanted to carry duration into payrolls.

def: Excess return per unit of volatility: portfolio return less the risk-free rate, divided by the standard deviation of returns. The standard yardstick for whether returns were earned or merely survived, though it punishes upside swings as harshly as downside ones and flatters strategies that sell tail risk — right up until the tail shows up.
usage: A Sharpe above one over a full cycle gets the desk's attention; above two gets its skepticism.

def: A dealer's standing interest in a particular security — the bond they need to sell or the block they're eager to buy, usually because inventory or a client order demands it. The axed dealer shows the most aggressive price on that side, which is exactly why the buy side asks around before trading. Also: the dominant market-maker in a given name.
usage: Find out who's axed in the bond before showing your order to the whole Street.

def: Two routes for the same dollar of cost: run it through the income statement now, or park it on the balance sheet and depreciate it over years. Capitalizing flatters current earnings and EBITDA without changing cash, which is why aggressive software-development capitalization is the first place a quality-of-earnings review looks.
usage: Half the margin expansion came from capitalizing developer salaries, so the desk rebuilt EBITDA on a fully expensed basis.

def: The workhorse model for the cost of equity: expected return equals the risk-free rate plus beta times the equity risk premium. Its logic — investors are paid only for market risk they cannot diversify away — earned a Nobel; its empirical record is shakier, but nothing has displaced it on the desk.
usage: Ask why the discount rate is 9.8% and the answer is CAPM, recited like scripture.

def: Contingent purchase price paid only if the target hits agreed milestones after closing — typically revenue or EBITDA targets over one to three years. A favorite bridge across valuation gaps, and a favorite source of post-closing litigation, since the buyer now controls the business being measured.
usage: The founders wanted certainty, so the sponsor capped the earnout at a quarter of headline value.

def: A portfolio company borrows fresh debt and hands the proceeds straight to its owners as a dividend — the sponsor pulls capital out without selling anything. It de-risks the fund's position early and flatters IRR, at the price of a releveraged balance sheet. Lenders tolerate it when cash flows are strong; critics call it eating the seed corn.
usage: Two years in, the sponsor ran a dividend recap and recovered most of its original check.

def: The layer between senior debt and equity — subordinated, expensive, and patient. Coupons run well into double digits, often part cash and part PIK, sometimes sweetened with warrants for an equity kicker. It fills the gap when senior lenders stop and the sponsor won't write a bigger check.
usage: With senior leverage capped at four turns, the sponsor filled the rest with mezzanine.

def: Dollar value of a basis point — how much a position's value changes when yields move one hundredth of a percent. It turns duration from an abstraction into a P&L number: a $10 million ten-year position with a DV01 of $9,000 loses that much every basis point yields back up.
usage: Risk flagged the book at $40,000 a basis point, so the trader trimmed DV01 before the auction.

def: The gap between the highest price a buyer will pay and the lowest a seller will accept — the market maker's compensation for standing ready to trade. Tight spreads mean liquidity; wide ones mean risk, thin volume, or both. It is a transaction cost investors pay whether or not it appears on any statement.
usage: The spread gapped out to forty cents on the open, so the desk sat on its hands until it tightened.

def: The volatility number that, plugged into an option-pricing model, reproduces the option's market price — the market's own forecast of how much the underlying will move, backed out rather than observed. It is quoted like a price because it effectively is one: buy options and you are long it, sell and you are short.
usage: Implied vol got crushed the morning after earnings, and the straddle buyers wore it.

def: The Federal Open Market Committee — twelve voters, comprising seven governors, the New York Fed president, and four rotating regional presidents — sets the fed funds target at eight scheduled meetings a year. The statement, the projections, and the chair's press conference each move markets separately, sometimes in opposite directions.
usage: Nothing else on the calendar matters this week; it's the FOMC statement at 2:00 and the presser at 2:30.

def: Return above what a portfolio's market exposure alone would have delivered — the residual after beta has taken its share. Genuinely scarce, zero-sum before fees and negative-sum after them, which is why most of what gets marketed as alpha turns out on inspection to be leverage, luck, or an unpriced risk factor.
usage: Strip out the small-cap tilt and the manager's alpha rounds to zero.

def: Selling immediately at the price a buyer is already showing, rather than waiting for someone to pay your offer. It concedes the bid-ask spread in exchange for certainty, which is why it signals urgency: the seller wants out now. When bids get hit repeatedly and reload lower, the tape is telling you something.
usage: By the third profit warning he stopped working the order and just hit the bid.

def: The slice of a consolidated subsidiary the parent doesn't own. Consolidation pulls in 100% of the subsidiary's results, so the outsiders' share sits in equity and gets carved out of net income as 'attributable to noncontrolling interests.' It is added to enterprise value so the multiple's numerator and denominator cover the same assets.
usage: The screen forgot the NCI, so every company with a partly owned subsidiary looked a turn cheaper than it was.

def: A stock's sensitivity to the broad market, measured as the slope of its returns regressed against the index. A beta of 1.2 means the shares tend to move 12% when the market moves 10% — the only risk CAPM pays you for, since everything idiosyncratic can be diversified away.
usage: The utility screens at a beta of 0.5, which is exactly why the fund parks there when things get loud.

def: The fee a target pays its jilted buyer for walking away — typically 2–4% of equity value — most often triggered when the board accepts a superior proposal. It compensates the buyer for sunk costs and deters frivolous topping bids without, courts insist, being so large it forecloses them.
usage: Any topping bidder has to clear the deal price plus a 3% breakup fee before the board will engage.

def: A smaller company purchased by an existing portfolio company rather than by the fund directly, folded into the platform to add products, geography, or customers. The arithmetic is the appeal: bolt-ons trade at lower multiples than the platform, so every acquired dollar of EBITDA gets marked up on consolidation. Serial bolt-ons are the engine of a roll-up.
usage: The platform closed its sixth bolt-on this year, each one financed off the existing credit facility.

def: Interest paid not in cash but in more debt — the coupon accretes to principal, and next period's interest compounds on the larger balance. It preserves liquidity for a stretched borrower, at the cost of a claim that grows while nobody writes a check. PIK toggles let the borrower choose each period.
usage: Two years of PIK quietly added a full turn of leverage to the balance sheet.

def: Short rates trading above long rates — the market pricing in cuts because it expects the economy to weaken. Every U.S. recession since the late 1960s has been preceded by one, though the lag is unreliable and the signal has cried wolf; it remains the most watched, most argued-over indicator in macro.
usage: The curve un-inverting is the part that historically precedes the recession, the strategist reminded the morning call.

def: A firm that continuously quotes both a bid and an offer, earning the spread in exchange for supplying liquidity and warehousing inventory risk. Modern equity market making is dominated by electronic firms that hold positions for seconds, hedge relentlessly, and profit on volume rather than opinion.
usage: When the headline hit, the market makers widened their quotes and half the book vanished in a blink.

def: Cboe's index of 30-day implied volatility on the S&P 500, computed from a strip of out-of-the-money option prices rather than any single contract. Quoted in annualized percentage points, it spikes when equities fall — hence the nickname 'fear gauge' — and is traded via futures and options, not the index itself.
usage: A 16 handle on the VIX had the desk selling premium into every rally.

def: The quarterly scatter in the Fed's Summary of Economic Projections, where every FOMC participant — nineteen when the board is full — marks the funds rate they judge appropriate at coming year-ends and over the longer run. The dots are anonymous, unweighted by vote, and routinely wrong; the median moves markets like a promise anyway.
usage: The median dot shifted up a quarter point, and the front end sold off before the chair finished speaking.

def: The standard deviation of the gap between a portfolio's returns and its benchmark's — how far the fund strays from the index it is measured against. Low tracking error is a virtue in an index fund and an indictment of an active one charging active fees for the privilege.
usage: Two percent tracking error against the benchmark means the manager is actually making bets, for better or worse.

def: Buying immediately at the seller's quoted price — the mirror image of hitting the bid. The buyer pays the spread for speed, and a stock where offers keep getting lifted is one where demand has grown impatient. Traders watch which side is being aggressed as a cleaner read on flow than price alone.
usage: Someone lifted every offer through the round number without flinching — that's not retail.

def: Book accounting and the tax code count income on different clocks, and deferred taxes reconcile them. Accelerated tax depreciation defers cash taxes and books a liability; losses and reserves recognized early for book purposes build an asset. Neither is cash — they are timing entries that unwind as the differences reverse.
usage: The model steps the DTL down as the accelerated depreciation schedule burns off.

def: Raw regression betas carry each company's capital structure inside them; unlevering strips the debt out to isolate pure business risk, and relevering applies the target's own structure. The Hamada formula — unlevered beta = levered beta / (1 + (1 - t) × D/E) — turns a peer set's betas into one clean beta for the target.
usage: Unlever the peers, relever at the target's structure — the associate can recite the drill in their sleep.

def: A negotiated window — usually 30 to 45 days after signing — during which the target may actively solicit competing bids, often with a reduced breakup fee for deals struck inside it. Common in sponsor take-privates where the board signed without a full auction and wants cover that the price was tested.
usage: The board skipped the auction and took a 40-day go-shop instead; nobody showed up, as usual.

def: The initial, foundational acquisition in a buy-and-build strategy — big enough to carry professional management, systems, and a credit facility that smaller targets can be bolted onto. Sponsors pay up for the platform on the theory that subsequent add-ons will blend the entry multiple down. Choose it badly and the whole roll-up inherits its flaws.
usage: The fund is hunting for a platform in specialty distribution before the add-on pipeline means anything.

def: Two philosophies of lender protection: maintenance covenants are tested every quarter whether or not the borrower does anything — leverage drifts above the line, you're in default. Incurrence covenants bite only when the borrower acts: raising new debt, paying a dividend. Bonds run on incurrence; loans historically ran on maintenance, until covenant-lite blurred the line.
usage: Only the revolver carries a maintenance test, and it springs only when drawings pass 35%.

def: The ten-year Treasury yield minus the two-year — the market's shorthand for curve shape. Positive and widening reads as growth and returning term premium; negative signals expected cuts and, historically, recession. Traders quote it in basis points and trade the spread itself through steepeners and flatteners rather than picking a point outright.
usage: 2s10s went out at minus thirty, its deepest close of the quarter.

def: Volume-weighted average price — total dollar volume traded divided by total shares, over a given window. It is the standard benchmark for execution quality: buy below VWAP and the desk did well. Large orders are routinely sliced by algorithms to track it, reinforcing the intraday volume curve they were built to follow.
usage: The client wanted the whole order worked to VWAP, no heroics.

def: The first derivative of an option's price with respect to the underlying: how many cents the option moves per dollar of stock. It doubles as a rough probability of finishing in the money and as the hedge ratio — a 40-delta call is offset with 40 shares short per contract.
usage: The book ran flat delta into the close, so the late selloff cost nothing but sleep.

def: Large-scale asset purchases — mostly Treasuries and agency mortgage-backed securities — that the Fed deploys once the policy rate hits zero and it still wants to ease. Paying for bonds with newly created reserves compresses term premia and long yields, pushing investors out the risk curve whether they like it or not.
usage: Every strategy deck from that era assumed QE would keep a lid on credit spreads.

def: The annual fee a fund skims off assets, quoted as a percentage and deducted daily whether performance shows up or not. One of the few variables in investing that is both known in advance and reliably predictive: costs compound against the investor exactly as returns compound for them.
usage: Eighty basis points a year is a high hurdle to clear before the manager has added anything.

def: Volume large enough to matter — enough that executing it moves the price, and enough that the counterparty asks questions. Trading in size distinguishes an institution repositioning from noise, and asking a dealer to show size tests whether the quote is real for more than a token amount. On the Street, size is credibility.
usage: The quote's fine for a hundred lots, but nobody will show size at that level.

def: Charges management would rather you ignore, added back to EBITDA on the theory they won't recur — severance, litigation, the founder's car lease. Legitimate in moderation, but marketed adjusted EBITDA in leveraged deals routinely runs 20–30% above the audited figure, and lenders spend diligence deciding which add-backs deserve to survive.
usage: Half the credit memo is a line-by-line ruling on which add-backs the sponsor gets to keep.

def: The extra return equities are expected to earn over the risk-free rate — the price of bearing market risk, and the most argued-over input in CAPM. Historical averages, survey figures, and implied estimates each give a different answer, usually somewhere between 4% and 6%; the choice quietly steers the whole valuation.
usage: The committee spent twenty minutes on the equity risk premium and settled, as committees do, on 5%.

def: A letter from an investment bank to the board stating that the consideration is fair, from a financial point of view, to the relevant shareholders — backed by DCF, comparables, and precedent transactions. It protects directors under the business judgment rule more than it prices the deal; the bank is paid either way.
usage: The special committee retained its own bank for the fairness opinion, given who was on the other side.

def: The purchase of a listed company by a sponsor, or its own management, that ends with the shares delisted and the ticker retired. Premiums to the undisturbed price, proxy votes, and fiduciary-duty litigation make it the most ceremonial form of LBO. The prize is running the business away from the glare of quarterly earnings.
usage: The board rejected the first take-private approach as opportunistic, then accepted a bid three dollars higher six weeks later.

def: A loan stripped of maintenance covenants, leaving lenders with incurrence-style protections borrowed from the bond market. Once a bull-market indulgence, now the standard — the overwhelming majority of institutional term loans price this way. Lenders lose the early tripwire; problems surface later, and recoveries have drifted lower accordingly.
usage: The deal cleared covenant-lite at par — nobody even asked for a leverage test.

def: A curve trade betting the gap between short and long yields will widen — long the front end against a short in the long end, weighted so parallel moves wash out. Bull steepeners come from the front end rallying on cut expectations; bear steepeners from the long end selling off on supply or inflation.
usage: The macro fund expressed its cutting-cycle view as a 2s10s steepener rather than an outright long.

def: A single trade large enough to move the market if handled carelessly — conventionally 10,000 shares or $200,000, in practice often far larger. Blocks are negotiated away from the open order book, through upstairs desks or dark venues, precisely so the size doesn't move the price before the trade prints.
usage: A 2-million-share block crossed at a 3% discount after the close, and the morning was spent guessing the seller.

def: The rate at which delta itself changes as the underlying moves — the second derivative of option price with respect to spot. Long options means long gamma: your hedge improves as the market moves, and you rebalance at a profit. Short gamma is the reverse, which is why sellers dread fast markets.
usage: Dealers were short gamma into expiry, and every downtick forced them to sell more.

def: QE run in reverse: the Fed shrinks its balance sheet by letting maturing bonds roll off without reinvestment, draining reserves from the banking system. It hums along in the background until it doesn't — the September 2019 repo squeeze showed what happens when reserves turn out to be scarcer than anyone modeled.
usage: The funding desk watches the reverse repo facility drain to gauge how much runway QT has left.

def: Investing a fixed sum at fixed intervals regardless of price, so more shares are bought when markets are cheap and fewer when they are dear. Mathematically it usually trails investing a lump sum immediately — markets drift upward — but behaviorally it gets money in the door, which lump sums often don't.
usage: The client couldn't stomach deploying the inheritance at once, so the plan averages it in over twelve months.

def: The qualitative intelligence around a move — who's been buying, why the bid feels soft, what the desk is hearing — as opposed to the numbers themselves. Salespeople trade in it, clients grade them on it, and good color is specific without naming names. 'Any color?' is the Street's polite way of asking what you actually know.
usage: The client didn't want a quote yet, just some color on why the front end backed up.

def: The diligence exercise that decides whether reported EBITDA is real, repeatable, and honestly cut. An outside accounting firm rebuilds earnings from the ledger — testing revenue recognition, normalizing one-offs, catching pull-forwards — and delivers a QoE report both sides negotiate from. Since the purchase price is a multiple of EBITDA, every adjustment moves real money.
usage: The QoE found a point of margin in pulled-forward shipments, and the price talk moved with it.

def: Everything the business is worth beyond the explicit forecast, collapsed into a single figure at the horizon and discounted back. Estimated by growing the final year's cash flow in perpetuity or by applying an exit multiple — and in most DCFs it carries 60–80% of the value, which should give anyone pause.
usage: Three-quarters of the value sits in the terminal, so the five-year forecast is mostly decoration.

def: A public offer to buy shares directly from a target's shareholders at a stated price, no merger vote required — the mechanical route for friendly two-step deals and hostile raids alike. U.S. offers run under the Williams Act: a minimum 20 business days open, with withdrawal rights and best-price rules.
usage: With the board stonewalling, the acquirer went directly to holders with an all-cash tender at a 30% premium.

def: The general partner's share of a fund's profits — customarily 20% — paid only after limited partners recover their capital and, usually, a preferred return. It is compensation structured as an investment gain, which is why it attracts capital-gains tax treatment and perennial political attention. Carry, not management fees, is where private equity fortunes are made.
usage: Junior partners get points of carry that won't pay out until the fund clears its hurdle years from now.

def: A mandatory prepayment clause that redirects a slice of excess cash flow — commonly 50%, stepping down as leverage falls — toward the term loan each year. It forces deleveraging in good years instead of letting cash pile up for dividends, and it's why LBO models pay down debt faster than the amortization schedule alone suggests.
usage: Model the sweep at 50% of excess cash flow, stepping to 25% below four turns.

def: One hundredth of a percentage point, written bp and pronounced "bip" — fixed income's atomic unit, because whole percentage points are far too blunt an instrument. A yield moving from 4.25% to 4.35% has risen ten basis points, and on a large book each one of them is real money.
usage: The deal priced twelve basis points inside initial talk, which told the syndicate desk everything about demand.

def: A private trading venue that hides orders until after they execute, letting institutions trade size without broadcasting intent to the wider market. Executions typically peg to the public midpoint, so dark pools borrow price discovery rather than contribute to it — the standing complaint from the lit exchanges that want the volume back.
usage: The algo worked a third of the order through dark pools before touching the lit exchanges.

def: The daily rent an option holder pays for time: the amount of premium that decays each day, all else held equal. It accelerates into expiry for at-the-money strikes, and it is the seller's whole business model — collecting theta while praying gamma doesn't collect first.
usage: Three quiet sessions of theta paid for the whole position before the stock finally moved.

def: Congress's twin instruction to the Fed, codified in 1977: maximum employment and stable prices. The statute technically lists a third goal — moderate long-term interest rates — which everyone politely ignores. The two mandates usually rhyme, but when inflation runs hot into a softening labor market, the committee must decide which miss it can better tolerate.
usage: With unemployment ticking up and core still sticky, both sides of the dual mandate are flashing at once.

def: Selling positions at a loss to bank a deduction against realized gains, then replacing them with similar — but not substantially identical — exposure to stay invested. The economics are a deferral, not a windfall: today's harvested loss lowers tomorrow's cost basis, so the tax bill is postponed rather than erased.
usage: December's selloff gave the desk a harvesting window it hadn't seen all year.

def: Advocating in public for positions you already hold — the fund manager praising a sector his fund happens to be long. Not quite dishonest, since conviction and position usually arrive together, but the disclosure discounts the analysis. The seasoned listener asks not whether someone is talking their book, but how large the book is.
usage: His bullish rates call landed softer once the audience realized he was talking his book.

def: A recent period's results annualized — the latest quarter times four, or the last month times twelve — to show where the business sits now rather than where it's been. Useful for fast growers whose trailing year understates them; treacherous for seasonal businesses, where annualizing the strong quarter manufactures growth that isn't there.
usage: The teaser quotes run-rate revenue, so the desk quietly checked which quarter got multiplied by four.

def: One of two ways to close a DCF: assume the final year's free cash flow grows at a modest rate g forever, so terminal value = FCF × (1 + g) / (WACC - g). The growth rate must sit at or below long-run nominal GDP growth — anything faster implies the company eventually becomes the economy.
usage: The desk sanity-checks the perpetuity growth answer against the exit multiple it implies, and vice versa.

def: A shareholder rights plan that floods the market with discounted stock for everyone except an acquirer who crosses an ownership trigger — typically 10–15% — diluting the raider into retreat. Pills are rarely swallowed; their point is to force negotiation with the board, and Delaware has blessed conventional pills for four decades.
usage: The board adopted a pill at 10% within days of the activist's 13D.

def: The two sides of every fund: the general partner manages the money, makes the investment decisions, and bears unlimited liability; the limited partners — pensions, endowments, sovereign funds — supply most of the capital and, by design, stay passive. The GP earns fees and carry; the LPs earn the returns net of both. The partnership agreement referees the rest.
usage: The LPs grumbled about fees at the annual meeting, but the GP's track record kept the re-ups coming.

def: The gap between a loan's face value and the price at which it's actually sold — paper issued at 98 hands lenders two points up front. Arrangers widen OID instead of the coupon when a deal struggles to clear, because a discount is paid once while a coupon lasts for the life of the loan.
usage: The buyside pushed for more OID, and the arrangers gave up a point rather than touch the spread.

def: The most recently auctioned Treasury of a given maturity is on-the-run; every older issue is off-the-run. The newest bond commands the deepest liquidity and so trades at a slightly lower yield — a premium arbitrageurs harvest by shorting the run against its cheaper predecessors, a trade that works until liquidity vanishes.
usage: The desk switched into the off-the-run to pick up three basis points, accepting the wider bid-ask.

def: Automatic trading halts triggered by outsized moves — market-wide pauses at 7% and 13% declines in the S&P 500, a full-day halt at 20%, plus single-stock halts when a name moves too far too fast. The point is not to stop the fall but to interrupt it, giving liquidity time to regroup and panic time to cool.
usage: The tape went dark at the level-one breaker, and the desk used the fifteen minutes to re-mark everything.

def: Sensitivity of an option's price to a one-point change in implied volatility — not an actual Greek letter, whatever the name suggests. Longer-dated and at-the-money options carry the most of it, which is why a vol repricing can move an option whose underlying hasn't budged.
usage: The desk trimmed vega ahead of the print rather than guess which way vol would break.

def: The unobservable interest rate — r-star in the literature — at which policy neither stimulates nor restrains an economy at full employment with stable inflation. Nobody can measure it, everyone estimates it anyway, and every argument about whether policy is "restrictive" is secretly an argument about where it sits.
usage: The hawks' case rests on neutral having drifted higher since the pandemic.

def: Buy back the same security — or one substantially identical — within thirty days of selling it at a loss, and the IRS disallows the deduction, folding it into the new position's basis instead. The window runs thirty days on either side of the sale, and it doesn't care which account the repurchase lands in.
usage: Swap into a different index fund for thirty-one days, or this year's deduction is disallowed.

def: Buying back shares you sold short, closing the position — at a profit if the price fell, at a loss if it didn't. Because covering is forced buying, it fuels the sharpest rallies: shorts scrambling to exit push the price into other shorts' stop levels. Many violent up-days are covering, not conviction.
usage: That afternoon rip was pure covering — the borrow got expensive and the shorts blinked first.

def: Financials restated as if a transaction had already happened — combining acquirer and target for a full year, layering in the new debt, sometimes crediting synergies not yet earned. The label has drifted: 'pro forma' now also covers any management-adjusted figure, so the first question is always which adjustments are inside.
usage: Leverage screens fine on pro forma EBITDA; on the audited number it's a covenant conversation.

def: The other way to close a DCF: value the business at the horizon by applying a market multiple — typically EV/EBITDA — to the final forecast year. Cleaner-looking than perpetuity math, but it smuggles relative valuation into an intrinsic exercise, so the implied growth rate deserves a check.
usage: An 11× exit multiple in year five is doing most of the work; the memo should say so.

def: The contractual escape hatch letting a buyer walk if the target suffers a material adverse change between signing and closing. Heavily negotiated carve-outs exclude market-wide and industry-wide shocks, so a valid MAC must be company-specific, durationally significant, and — per Delaware case law — nearly impossible to prove.
usage: The buyer's counsel floated a MAC argument; the target's counsel reminded them how that usually ends in Delaware.

def: Capital that limited partners have committed but the fund has not yet called or invested — the industry's stockpile of purchasing power. It earns nothing until deployed, and the management-fee clock is running, so aging dry powder pressures sponsors to transact. Record levels of it are the usual explanation for rich entry multiples.
usage: With two years left in the investment period and half the fund still dry powder, the sponsor got less picky.

def: Compensation for lenders when a borrower repays early and takes their yield away. High-yield bonds carry hard non-call periods followed by declining premiums; leveraged loans usually settle for six or twelve months of 101 soft call, and only against repricings. The tighter the protection, the more an early refinancing costs.
usage: The repricing waits until the 101 soft call rolls off next quarter.

def: Treasuries whose principal adjusts with CPI, the fixed coupon paid on that adjusted amount — so the quoted yield is a real yield. At maturity the holder receives the greater of adjusted or original principal. The tax wrinkle: the inflation accretion is taxed annually as income even though it isn't paid until the end.
usage: With breakevens near two and a half, the allocator rotated part of the nominal sleeve into TIPS.

def: The first day a stock trades without entitlement to its next declared dividend; buy on or after it and the payment goes to the seller. The share price notionally opens lower by the dividend amount — arithmetic, not sentiment — which routinely fools screens flagging the apparent drop.
usage: Half the morning's apparent losers were just names trading ex-div, so the desk struck them from the alert list.

def: The 1973 formula that prices a European option from five inputs — spot, strike, time, rate, and volatility — by arguing a continuously hedged portfolio must earn the risk-free rate. Four inputs are observable; the fifth, volatility, is where all the disagreement lives, which is why traders quote options in vol rather than dollars.
usage: Nobody on the desk believes Black-Scholes literally, but everyone still quotes and hedges in its language.

def: The ornithology of central banking: hawks prioritize the inflation fight and lean toward tighter policy, doves weigh employment and lean toward easier. Most officials migrate between camps as the data shift, which is why markets grade every speech on the hawk–dove scale before the full text is even read.
usage: The minutes read more hawkish than the statement, and the two-year backed up six basis points.

def: The legal obligation to put the client's interests ahead of the adviser's own — a higher bar than suitability, which only asks whether a product was defensible, not whether it was best. The distinction shows up in the fee line: fiduciaries are typically paid by the client, not by the products they recommend.
usage: Ask whether the person across the table is a fiduciary before asking anything else.

def: Taking the other side of a move or a consensus — selling a rally you think is overdone, buying a panic you think is mechanical. The bet is that the crowd has overreacted and prices will revert. Done with discipline it harvests overreaction; done stubbornly it's how traders get carried out.
usage: The desk faded the opening gap and was flat by lunch with the day's P&L made.

def: The audited numbers versus management's preferred version of them. Non-GAAP measures strip items the company deems non-core — stock compensation, amortization, restructuring — and the SEC requires a reconciliation and at least equal prominence for the GAAP figure. The gap between the two is a running gauge of how hard a company is selling its story.
usage: The gap between GAAP and adjusted EPS has widened four straight years, which the desk reads as a tell.

def: A timing fix for the DCF's fiction that all cash arrives on December 31. Since cash actually flows through the year, each period is discounted at 0.5, 1.5, 2.5 years instead of 1, 2, 3 — a small adjustment that nudges value up a few percent.
usage: Someone will ask whether the model uses the mid-year convention; the answer should be yes, and it should be footnoted.

def: The normalized level of working capital a buyer expects to receive at closing, negotiated into the purchase agreement; deliver more and the price adjusts up, less and it adjusts down. Usually set off a trailing twelve-month average, and a reliable source of post-closing disputes over what counts as 'normal.'
usage: Seller ran receivables hard into close, so the true-up against the peg clawed back part of the price.

def: The formal notice a fund sends its limited partners demanding a slice of their committed capital, usually on ten business days' notice, to fund a deal or pay fees. Commitments are promises, not deposits — the money stays with LPs until called. Missing one triggers default provisions harsh enough that almost nobody ever does.
usage: The capital call landed the same week the endowment was scrambling for liquidity, which is how it always goes.

def: The most expensive exit in the book: to redeem early, the issuer pays the present value of all remaining coupons and principal, discounted at Treasuries plus a thin spread — often 50 basis points. When rates have fallen, that discount rate makes the payment punishing, which is precisely the point. It protects investment-grade bondholders in practice, not just theory.
usage: Retiring the notes early means paying the make-whole at Treasuries plus 50 — the CFO winced.

def: The nominal Treasury yield minus the TIPS real yield at the same maturity — the inflation rate at which the two bonds return the same. It doubles as the market's inflation forecast, though liquidity and risk premia contaminate the reading, so a 2.4% ten-year breakeven is expectation plus noise, not prophecy.
usage: Five-year breakevens gapped ten basis points on the CPI print, doing the Fed's commentary for it.

def: A company purchasing its own shares, shrinking the count and mechanically lifting earnings per share. Done below intrinsic value it transfers wealth to the remaining holders; done at the top of the cycle it is expensive financial cosmetics. Either way it returns cash with more flexibility — and less commitment — than a dividend.
usage: Management leaned on the buyback to hit the EPS guide, and the analysts noticed within the hour.

def: The arbitrage identity binding European options to their underlying: call minus put equals spot minus the present value of the strike, or C – P = S – PV(K). Break it and a riskless profit appears, so it holds tightly in liquid markets — and it means any position can be built synthetically from the other pieces.
usage: Parity was a touch loose at the open, and the arb desk closed it before coffee.

def: The rare feat of bringing inflation back to target through tighter policy without tipping the economy into recession. History offers few clean examples — 1995 is the canonical one — because monetary policy's long lags make the runway hard to judge until the wheels have either touched down or buckled.
usage: Every strategist's base case is a soft landing, which is precisely what makes the desk nervous.

def: The peak-to-trough decline in a portfolio's value, usually quoted as the worst one on record. It measures the pain an investor actually experiences — a 50% drawdown requires a 100% gain just to get back to even, which is why the arithmetic of losses is crueler than the arithmetic of gains.
usage: The backtest looks heroic until you notice the 40% drawdown buried in year three.

def: A brief, reflexive rally in something still fundamentally falling — short covering and bargain hunters producing a bounce that fails and rolls over to new lows. The grim etymology: even a dead cat bounces if dropped from a great height. The trouble is that genuine bottoms and dead cats look identical for the first few days.
usage: Up eight percent on no news after a sixty percent slide — the desk called it a dead cat.

def: Shares a company has bought back and holds in its own vault — carried as a negative in equity, stripped of votes and dividends. Heavy repurchasers can push total book equity negative without implying distress. The name also lends itself to the treasury stock method, which counts option dilution net of the cash exercise would raise.
usage: Under the treasury stock method the underwater options drop out of the diluted count entirely.

def: The partial year between the valuation date and the next fiscal year-end, discounted for its actual fraction rather than a full period. Valuing a company in July means the remaining half-year of cash flow gets a discount period of roughly 0.5 — or 0.25 under the mid-year convention.
usage: The model runs off a June valuation date, so there's a stub period before the first full forecast year.

def: The standard convention for private deals: the seller keeps the cash and pays off the debt, so the buyer purchases the enterprise, not the capital structure. Headline enterprise value bridges to equity proceeds by subtracting net debt and adjusting for the working capital peg — where most of the real negotiating happens.
usage: The bid is cash-free, debt-free, so the fight over what counts as a debt-like item is the real negotiation.

def: The contractual order in which exit proceeds flow: return of LP capital first, then the preferred return, then a GP catch-up, then the 80/20 profit split. European waterfalls settle up at the fund level; American ones go deal by deal, paying carry sooner. Every clause exists because someone once got paid in the wrong order.
usage: Model the waterfall before you celebrate the exit — the headline number and the LPs' check are different animals.

def: An investment-grade issuer downgraded into junk, dragging its bonds across the BBB–/BB+ line and out of index-tracking portfolios that can't hold them. The forced selling often overshoots fundamentals, which is why fallen angels have historically outperformed bonds born high-yield. The crossing itself, not the credit, creates the trade.
usage: The desk bought the fallen angel a week after the downgrade, once the index sellers were done.

def: The overnight plumbing of finance: one party sells securities and agrees to repurchase them tomorrow at a slightly higher price, the difference being interest on what is functionally a collateralized loan. Trillions roll through it daily, funding dealer inventories and levered Treasury trades — invisible until it seizes, as it did in September 2019.
usage: When repo printed above the funds rate for a week, the funding desk knew cash, not collateral, was the scarce commodity.

def: The contractual period — typically 180 days after an IPO — during which insiders and pre-IPO holders may not sell. Its expiry is a calendar event traders mark well in advance: a wall of potential supply meets a float that was, until that morning, artificially thin. Underwriters can waive it early, and sometimes do.
usage: With the lock-up expiring Thursday, the desk expected the borrow to loosen and the shorts to press.

def: Selling a call against stock you already own: the premium is income, the cost is surrendering any upside beyond the strike. It converts a holding into a yield trade that works best in flat markets — and it caps exactly the rallies that make owning equities worthwhile, a trade-off the brochure rarely leads with.
usage: The wealth team wrote covered calls over the concentrated position while the client decided whether to sell.

def: A diffusion index distilled from purchasing managers' monthly answers to simple questions — are new orders, output, employment, and supplier deliveries better or worse than last month? Readings above 50 signal expansion, below 50 contraction, and because the survey lands ahead of the hard data it serves as the cycle's early thermometer. ISM publishes the classic U.S. versions; S&P Global runs the global family.
usage: Manufacturing PMI slipped below 50 again, yet cyclicals caught a bid — the bad-news-is-good-news trade lives on.

def: How closely two assets move together, scored from -1 to +1. Diversification lives or dies on it, and its cruelest property is instability: correlations that sat near zero for a decade have a habit of lurching toward one in a crisis, precisely when the diversification was supposed to pay off.
usage: Stocks and bonds spent the year moving together, which rather undermined the point of owning both.

def: A rally that accelerates as it goes, driven less by improving fundamentals than by the fear of not being in it — underinvested managers chasing, shorts covering, dealers hedging into strength. Melt-ups feel euphoric and tend to arrive late in a cycle; the term carries a warning that the same reflexivity works in reverse.
usage: Career risk does the buying in a melt-up — nobody wants to explain holding cash at year-end.

def: Revenue when it's earned, expenses when they're incurred — regardless of when cash actually moves. The matching principle pairs costs with the sales they produce, which makes earnings a better gauge of performance and a worse gauge of liquidity. It's the whole reason the cash flow statement exists: to undo the accruals.
usage: Earnings looked fine; the accruals were doing the work, and the cash flow statement told on them.

def: The cash the business generates for all capital providers before any financing effects: EBIT × (1 - tax rate), plus D&A, less capex and the change in working capital. Because it ignores the capital structure, it pairs with WACC and discounts to enterprise value rather than equity value.
usage: Interest expense never touches unlevered free cash flow — the first thing the desk checks in a borrowed model.

def: The confidential information memorandum — the sell-side's book-length marketing document, packed with business overview, financials, and management's adjusted-EBITDA case, distributed to buyers who have signed NDAs. Written to be persuasive rather than neutral; diligence exists to find out which parts survive contact with the data room.
usage: The CIM shows a hockey stick in year two; flag it for the diligence list.

def: Multiple on invested capital: total value returned and remaining, divided by the money put in — a 2.5× MOIC means every dollar became two-fifty. It ignores time entirely, which is both its virtue and its flaw; a 2× in three years and a 2× in ten look identical. Read it alongside IRR, never instead.
usage: The deal returned a respectable MOIC, but seven years of holding turned the IRR into an apology.

def: New money lent to a company already in Chapter 11, blessed by the bankruptcy court with superpriority status — it gets repaid ahead of nearly everyone, sometimes priming existing liens. Because the estate can't survive without it, DIP lenders extract fat economics and tight milestones, and often steer the whole case.
usage: The existing lenders offered the DIP themselves rather than let new money prime their collateral.

def: The Secured Overnight Financing Rate — a volume-weighted median of overnight Treasury repo transactions, published each morning by the New York Fed. It replaced LIBOR as the dollar benchmark after the rigging scandals, swapping survey-based guesses for actual trades; nearly every new floating-rate loan, swap, and note now resets off it.
usage: The term loan resets quarterly at SOFR plus 250, so treasury rebuilt its hedges as the benchmark drifted higher.

def: An over-allotment option letting underwriters sell up to 15% more shares in an IPO than the base deal, then either buy them back in the market to support a wobbly price or exercise the option to cover if the deal trades up. It is the syndicate's shock absorber, named for the first company to grant one.
usage: The stock hugged the offer price for a week — the greenshoe doing its quiet stabilizing work.

def: A put bought against a long stock position — insurance with a visible premium and a hard floor at the strike, less what you paid. The position keeps its upside, which distinguishes it from simply selling; the recurring cost is why most investors buy protection only after the event they feared.
usage: Ahead of the lockup expiry, the fund put a three-month floor under its largest holding.

def: The arithmetic quirk that moves year-over-year figures even when nothing new happened: an anomaly twelve months ago drops out of the comparison and the growth rate jumps or sags. Analysts who ignore them get whipsawed; analysts who respect them read the annualized three- and six-month rates instead.
usage: Half the expected disinflation this spring is just base effects rolling off last year's energy spike.

def: The curve of portfolios offering the highest expected return for each level of risk — Markowitz's contribution, and the reason allocation is treated as an optimization problem rather than a stock-picking contest. Everything below the curve is leaving return on the table; everything above it is a typo in the spreadsheet.
usage: The optimizer says the current mix sits well inside the frontier, mostly because cash is doing nothing.

def: The moment holders stop debating and simply sell — indiscriminately, at any price, on heavy volume — because the pain has finally beaten the thesis. Classic bottoms are made this way, since once the last reluctant seller is out, supply exhausts itself. The catch: real capitulation is only ever certain in hindsight.
usage: Volume tripled and everything went out on the lows — if that wasn't capitulation, it auditioned well.

def: The rules governing when a sale becomes revenue — under ASC 606, when the company satisfies a performance obligation by transferring control, at a price allocated across everything promised. Multi-year contracts, bundles, and licenses make the timing genuinely judgmental, which is why revenue recognition is where most accounting scandals start.
usage: Diligence flagged that management recognizes the full license up front while the peer set spreads it over the term.

def: Valuation by peer group: gather comparable public companies, compute their multiples — EV/EBITDA, P/E, EV/revenue — and apply the range to the target's metrics. Fast, market-grounded, and only as good as the peer set; the real skill is deciding who actually belongs in it.
usage: Half the comp set trades at a premium for reasons the target can't claim, which is rather the point of screening.

def: The virtual repository where the seller posts contracts, financials, org charts, and litigation files for buyer diligence, with access logged down to the page view. Sellers stage sensitive material in waves — the crown jewels appear late, once the field has narrowed and exclusivity is close.
usage: Legal flagged three change-of-control clauses buried in the data room's customer contracts folder.

def: Distributions to paid-in capital: the cash a fund has actually returned to LPs, divided by the cash they contributed — realized money, not marks. Below 1.0×, LPs have taken out less cash than they put in, whatever the remaining portfolio is marked at. In a slow exit market it becomes the only multiple allocators trust.
usage: The fund's paper marks look heroic, but the LPs keep asking the same question: where's the DPI?

def: The instrument where enterprise value runs out — the class that is partly in the money and therefore likely to be handed the equity in a restructuring. Everything senior gets paid; everything junior gets wiped; the fulcrum gets the keys. Finding it is the whole game in distressed investing.
usage: The fund built its position in the second lien, betting that's where the fulcrum sits.

def: A bond stripped to a single payment: no coupons, sold at a deep discount, redeemed at face. All the return arrives as accretion, which makes Macaulay duration equal maturity exactly and price swings brutal at the long end. The IRS taxes the phantom accrual annually, which is why zeros live happily in retirement accounts.
usage: The pension matched its 2045 liability with long zeros and stopped worrying about reinvestment altogether.

def: A capital raise offered first to existing shareholders, pro rata and at a discount, so they can maintain their stake or sell the entitlement to someone who will. Standard practice in Europe, rarer in the U.S., and often read as distress when the discount is deep and the underwriting fee is fat.
usage: The rights came at a 35% discount, which told the desk everything about the balance sheet before the release did.

def: A call and a put bought on the same strike and expiry — a pure bet that the underlying moves more than the market has priced, direction immaterial. The premium paid sets the breakevens on either side; sell the same package and you are betting on quiet, with the loss unbounded.
usage: The straddle was pricing an 8% earnings move, and the stock obliged with nine.

def: The difference between more dollars and more stuff — nominal figures come in current prices, real figures strip inflation out. The distinction bites hardest in rates: a 5% yield against 4% inflation pays a real 1%, and it is the real rate, not the sticker, that does the economic restraining.
usage: Nominal GDP looked heroic; deflate it and the real print barely cleared one percent.

def: The classic balanced allocation — 60% stocks for growth, 40% bonds for ballast — that served as the industry's default for half a century. Its logic rests on bonds cushioning equity selloffs, an assumption that works beautifully in disinflationary regimes and failed spectacularly in 2022, when both sides fell double digits together.
usage: Every few years someone declares the 60/40 dead, and every few years it quietly outlives the eulogy.

def: The market's binary mood switch: risk-on, and equities, credit, emerging markets, and carry trades rally together while havens sag; risk-off, and money runs the other way, into Treasuries, the dollar, the yen, and gold. When the regime dominates, correlations across risk assets converge toward one and single-name analysis matters less than the mood itself.
usage: It was a pure risk-off tape — nothing idiosyncratic worked, and the ten-year did all the talking.

def: Operating profit with depreciation and amortization added back — a rough, capital-structure-neutral proxy for cash generation, and the denominator of choice for leverage ratios and deal multiples. Its famous flaw is what it ignores: capex, working capital, and taxes are all real cash, which is why lenders paper covenants around a defined version.
usage: The bid is 11× EBITDA, but the real debate is whose EBITDA — reported, adjusted, or the QoE number.

def: Comps built from deals rather than trading prices: multiples paid in past acquisitions of similar companies, control premium included. They tend to run higher than trading comps for exactly that reason — and they age badly, since a multiple paid at the top of a cycle says little about today.
usage: The precedents top out the football field, as precedents do — someone paid for control in a hotter market.

def: A mechanism in stock-for-stock deals that bounds what target shareholders receive when the acquirer's share price moves between signing and closing. Fixed-ratio deals may collar the implied value; fixed-value deals collar the share count — either way, someone stops bearing market risk once the price exits the band.
usage: The acquirer's stock slid to the bottom of the collar, so the exchange ratio hit its ceiling.

def: A vehicle the sponsor raises to buy an asset from its own aging fund, letting existing LPs cash out or roll while the GP keeps managing the company. It solves the problem of a fund's clock expiring before a good asset's story does. Since the GP sits on both sides of the price, fairness opinions and LP votes do the refereeing.
usage: Rather than sell its best asset into a soft market, the GP moved it into a continuation fund.

def: The treaty that keeps a layered capital structure civil: it fixes lien priority between first and second lien lenders, sets payment waterfalls, and muzzles juniors with standstill periods before they can enforce. Nobody reads it closely until the credit sours — then it becomes the most important document in the room.
usage: Check the intercreditor before assuming the second lien can block the sale — the standstill runs 180 days.

def: The extra yield a corporate bond pays over a Treasury of matching maturity — compensation for default risk, downgrade risk, and thinner liquidity. Quoted in basis points, it is the market's running verdict on a borrower's health: spreads gap wider when fear rises and grind tighter when money is easy and complacent.
usage: Investment-grade spreads sat inside a hundred basis points, which the strategist read as priced for perfection.

def: The quarterly disclosure institutional managers with over $100 million in U.S. equities must file with the SEC, revealing long positions as of quarter-end — up to 45 days stale, shorts excluded, and derivatives only partially visible. Copying them is a popular hobby; the lag and the omissions are why it rarely works as advertised.
usage: Forty-five days stale or not, the desk still combed the 13Fs to see who had been building the position.

def: A protective put financed by selling a call above the market: downside floored, upside capped, often for zero net premium. Corporates and concentrated shareholders use it to defer a sale without wearing the risk — the classic way to hedge a founder's stake without a headline-making disposal.
usage: The banker pitched a zero-cost collar so the founder could hedge without booking a sale.

def: The macro trap of the 1970s: stagnant growth and rising unemployment paired with stubborn inflation, a combination the era's Phillips curve said shouldn't happen. It corners the central bank — easing feeds the inflation, tightening deepens the slump — and it punishes stocks and bonds at the same time, leaving few places to hide.
usage: One hot CPI print in a soft payrolls month and the stagflation notes start writing themselves.

def: The schedule by which a target-date fund shifts from stocks toward bonds as the investor ages — steep equity early, ballast late. The design question is whether the path runs 'to' retirement or 'through' it: through-paths keep more equity past the retirement date, betting longevity risk outweighs sequence risk.
usage: The fund's glide path parks retirees at 45% equity, which is bolder than most of them realize.

def: The Street's two tribes of flow: real money — pensions, insurers, mutual funds — unlevered and slow-moving, buying to hold; fast money — hedge funds, CTAs, prop desks — levered, tactical, and capable of reversing by lunch. Dealers care which one is doing the buying, because real-money flows tend to persist and fast-money flows tend to stampede.
usage: The rally has legs if real money is behind it; if it's fast money, fade the third day.

def: The multiplier fixed costs put on profit swings. A business that covers its overhead sees each incremental revenue dollar fall to earnings at a far higher margin — and the same math runs in reverse when sales slip. High operating leverage makes earnings estimates volatile and small revenue misses expensive.
usage: Margins expanded 400 basis points on 8% revenue growth — operating leverage doing exactly what the bull case promised.

def: The housekeeping that makes comps comparable: restating companies with different fiscal year-ends onto a common twelve-month calendar, usually by weighting adjacent fiscal years. A June year-end and a December year-end are describing different economies; comparing their raw multiples without adjustment quietly bakes in the gap.
usage: Two of the comps have June year-ends, so the analyst spent the morning calendarizing before anyone saw a multiple.

def: The structural fork in private transactions: buy the entity's shares and inherit everything, known and unknown, or buy specified assets and assume only the liabilities you name. Buyers prefer asset deals for the tax basis step-up and the liability screen; sellers prefer stock deals to avoid double taxation and hand off the whole company cleanly.
usage: Tax counsel pushed for an asset deal until the contract-assignment consents made it impractical.

def: A direct stake in a single deal offered to select LPs alongside the fund's own position, typically fee-free and carry-free. LPs prize it as the cheapest exposure in private equity; GPs use it to stretch into deals bigger than the fund alone could underwrite. The catch is adverse selection — and the concentration risk LPs signed up to avoid.
usage: The pension took the co-invest mostly to average down its fee load across the relationship.

def: A negotiated push of a loan's maturity, bought with a fatter spread and an amendment fee, so the borrower avoids refinancing into a hostile market. Sensible when the credit merely needs time; derided as “amend and pretend” when it just postpones a reckoning the balance sheet cannot escape.
usage: Lenders took the amend and extend — 75 basis points and a fee beat testing the refi market.

def: A bond the issuer can redeem early at a set price, typically once rates have fallen far enough to refinance cheaper. The holder has sold the issuer an option and collects extra yield for it — hence the bond appreciates grudgingly in rallies, its price pinned near the call. Yield-to-worst is the honest measure.
usage: The desk quoted it to worst, since at this dollar price the call date is the real maturity.

def: A U.S.-listed certificate representing shares of a foreign company held in custody by a depositary bank, letting American investors buy overseas names in dollars, on domestic exchanges, with U.S. settlement. Ratios vary — one ADR may bundle several underlying shares or a fraction of one — and sponsored programs carry the issuer's cooperation while unsponsored ones do not.
usage: The ADR trades at a persistent discount to the local line, which the arb desk regards as rent.

def: The same promise — buy or sell an asset at a set price on a future date — in two wrappers. Futures are exchange-traded, standardized, and marked to market daily through a clearinghouse; forwards are private, bespoke, and settle once at maturity, leaving each side carrying the other's credit risk until then.
usage: The treasurer wanted a forward for the odd date; the desk hedged it with the listed future.

def: A fixed-weight gauge of the dollar against six currencies, with the euro alone carrying nearly 58% of the basket. DXY's composition has changed just once since its 1973 start — the euro absorbing five legacy currencies in 1999 — which makes it less a trade-weighted measure than a euro mirror, yet the desk still quotes it as shorthand for global dollar strength.
usage: Guidance cuts from the multinationals pile up whenever the dollar index grinds through its highs.

def: What was paid for an asset, adjusted for splits, reinvested dividends, and return of capital — the number the IRS subtracts from sale proceeds to compute the taxable gain. Heirs receive a step-up to market value at death, which is why appreciated stock is often worth more held than sold.
usage: Twenty years of reinvested dividends left the client's basis scattered across forty tax lots.

def: The unofficial earnings expectation circulating among traders — the number the stock actually needs to beat, as distinct from the published analyst consensus. It explains why shares can fall on a headline beat: the whisper was higher. It appears on no official screen, which is precisely what makes it worth knowing.
usage: Consensus was ninety cents, but the whisper was a dollar, and the stock traded like everyone knew it.

def: Days of inventory plus days of receivables, minus the days a company takes to pay suppliers — the stretch of time cash spends trapped in operations. Shorter is cheaper to fund; a negative cycle means customers and suppliers finance the business, which is why some retailers grow without consuming a dime of working capital.
usage: Every day shaved off the cash conversion cycle frees working capital the sponsor would rather use for the dividend.

def: The one-page chart that lays every valuation method's range side by side — trading comps, precedents, DCF, LBO — as horizontal bars, so the reader can see where the answers cluster. Its quiet job is triangulation; its quieter job is showing which method the banker leaned on to hit the number.
usage: The offer lands above every bar on the football field — a chart that argues for the seller.

def: The preliminary agreement sketching price, structure, and process before either side spends serious money on diligence and drafting. Mostly non-binding — except for exclusivity, confidentiality, and expenses — but it sets the anchor: buyers who sign an LOI at one number and retrade at another need a documented reason.
usage: The LOI grants sixty days of exclusivity, so the diligence clock is already running.

def: The hurdle — usually 8% a year — that limited partners must earn on their capital before the general partner sees a dollar of carry. It compounds from each capital call until cash comes back, so long holds quietly raise the bar. A relic of a higher-rate era that has proven remarkably resistant to negotiation.
usage: The fund finally cleared its pref in year five, and the catch-up provision did the rest.

def: Debt rated below BBB– (or Baa3), carrying a coupon fat enough to compensate for real default risk. Unlike leveraged loans, high-yield bonds are typically unsecured, run on incurrence covenants only, and pay fixed rather than floating — a lock the issuer blesses when rates rise and rues when they fall. “Junk” is the older, more honest name.
usage: The issuer paired a term loan with $500 million of high-yield notes to term out the bridge.

def: Pools of residential mortgages guaranteed by Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, or Ginnie Mae, so the credit risk is governmental — explicit for Ginnie, implied for the other two — and the real risk is prepayment. Homeowners refinance when rates fall and sit tight when they rise, handing investors their cash back at the worst possible moments — the negative convexity that defines the sector.
usage: As rates dropped, the MBS book shortened underneath the hedgers and the whole street chased duration at once.

def: The fundamental trade-off in execution: a limit order names your price and risks not trading, while a market order guarantees the fill and risks the price. Market orders pay the spread and eat whatever depth the book offers; limit orders supply liquidity but can watch the market run away without them.
usage: In a name this thin you use limits, full stop — a market order here is a donation.

def: The two postures of a futures curve. In contango, later contracts trade above spot — storage, financing, and calm expectations — so long positions bleed as each contract rolls down toward spot. Backwardation inverts it: futures below spot signal scarcity now, and rolling longs collect the difference instead of paying it.
usage: Crude slipped into steep backwardation, and the roll yield quietly did half the fund's work.

def: The money other countries hold their savings in: central banks stockpile it, commodities price in it, and cross-border debts settle in it. The dollar's roughly 58% share of global reserves buys the U.S. cheaper borrowing and sanctions leverage — the "exorbitant privilege" France's finance ministry was already grumbling about in the 1960s.
usage: Every few years a de-dollarization thesis makes the rounds; the reserve share data barely budges.

def: Spreading capital across assets that don't fail together, so no single position can sink the portfolio. Famously the only free lunch in finance — imperfectly correlated holdings lower risk without lowering expected return — though the lunch gets thin when everything is expensive and correlations converge at the worst possible moment.
usage: Owning five tech funds is not diversification; it is the same bet in five wrappers.

def: Already reflected in the market price, so the news itself moves nothing when it arrives. Markets discount expectations rather than events — which is why a stock can fall on good results everyone anticipated and rally on bad ones less awful than feared.
